coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of B,N-doped CQDs as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of cobalt ions in environmental water and biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xinyi
Wang, Lumin
Liu, Qi
Chen, Miao
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as versatile fluorescence probes for simultaneous detection of cobalt ion (Co2+) in various samples is still unmet. Herein, novel boron, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (B,N-CQDs) were facile synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method, in which 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and ethylenediamine were selected as precursors. Owing to the unique properties of the precursors, the obtained B,N-CQDs exhibited excellent recognition capability of Co2+, favorable biocompatibility, and high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY, 64.8%), which enabled the B,N-CQDs as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of Co2+. The response of the probes to Co2+ was linear in two ranges of 0.5–10 and 10–2000 nM with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.52 nM. Feasibility of practical application of the probes was assessed by quantitative detection of trace Co2+ in real water samples including sewage water, river water and tap water. Furthermore, Co2+ in human urine was detected by using the probes with recoveries between 96% and 104%, indicating that the established probes could meet the requirements of practical detection in urine samples. Besides, the probes were successfully applied to monitoring Co2+ in A549 cells via fluorescence imaging attributed to their excellent photostability and high signal-to-background ratio. All these results show that the designed B,N-CQDs are ideal probes for simultaneously detecting Co2+ in environmental water and biological samples, which would expand the practical application of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Cobalt ions
Versatile fluorescence probes
Sensitive detection
Water samples
Biological samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Co2+ by the probes
The selectivity and anti-interference assay of the probes
Determination of Co2+ in real samples
Optimization of detection conditions of the probes
Detection of Co2+ by the probes
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the probes for detection of Co2+
Detection of Co2+ in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878339, 21904141), the Key R&D Project in the industrial field funded by Hunan Provincial Science &Technology Department, China (2019GK2131), and Independent Exploration and Innovation Project for Graduate Students of Central South University, China (2020zzts055).

We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878339, 21904141), the Key R&D Project in the industrial field funded by Hunan Provincial Science &Technology Department, China (2019GK2131), and Independent Exploration and Innovation Project for Graduate Students of Central South University, China (2020zzts055).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM image (inset view) of B,N-CQDs; (b) The size distribution of B,N-CQDs. High resolution XPS of C 1 s (c), O 1 s (d), N 1 s (c) and B 1 s (f) of B,N-CQDs. (g) FT-IR spectrum of 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (a, purple line), ethylenediamine (b, green line) and B,N-CQDs (c, brown line). (h) UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of B,N-CQDs.

(a) FL spectra of the B,N-CQDs response to Co2+ with different concentrations; [B,N-CQDs] = 0.50 mg·mL−1; pH 7.0 of PBS (0.001 M); (b) and (c) Linear relationship between the concentration of Co2+ and the degree of fluorescent quenching (F0-F), F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of B,N-CQDs at 509 nm in the presence or absence of Co2+, respectively. (d) Selectivity for various metal ions (1 mM). (e) Fluorescence decay traces of B,N-CQDs and with addition of different concentration of Co2+ solution; (f) Absorption spectra and fluorescent spectra of B,N-CQDs, Co2+, B,N-CQDs-Co2+ system, and summed UV–vis absorption spectrum between B,N-CQDs and Co2+, respectively.

(a) Effects of B,N-CQDs at different concentrations on the viability of A549 cells for 24 h. Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells treated with B,N-CQDs (200 µg mL−1) and different concentration of Co2+ (0, 1 µM and 1 mM) for 24 h. Images were taken under bright field (b, e, h), fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm (c, f, i), and merge of corresponding bright field and fluorescence images (d, g, j) (scale bar = 50 µm).
Schematic representation of preparation, analysis and application of the B,N-CQDs.
Comparison of detection performance of different fluorescence probes for Co2+ detection.
Determination of Co2+ in real water samples.

found
black
) refers to the concentration of Co2 + detected in non-spiked real samples. So, when “added” is zero, “found” is equal to
found

concerning Yudai river of Changsha and Industrial Wastewater (diluted 5-fold), which are 1250 and 452 (diluted 5-fold) nM respectively. But for tap water of laboratory, when “added” is zero, the concentration of Co2 + could not been detected, that is, “
found
black
” is zero. “–” means no found.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Co2+ in real human urine samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: In Table 3, NO. 1,2,3 midstream human urine samples were collected from three healthy volunteers (50-year old male, 25-year-old male and 22-year-old female), respectively. “–” means no found.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of B,N-doped CQDs as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of cobalt ions in environmental water and biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Central South University, Changsha 410013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Central South University, Changsha 410013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as versatile fluorescence probes for simultaneous detection of cobalt ion (Co2+) in various samples is still unmet. Herein, novel boron, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (B,N-CQDs) were facile synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method, in which 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and ethylenediamine were selected as precursors. Owing to the unique properties of the precursors, the obtained B,N-CQDs exhibited excellent recognition capability of Co2+, favorable biocompatibility, and high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY, 64.8%), which enabled the B,N-CQDs as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of Co2+. The response of the probes to Co2+ was linear in two ranges of 0.5–10 and 10–2000 nM with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.52 nM. Feasibility of practical application of the probes was assessed by quantitative detection of trace Co2+ in real water samples including sewage water, river water and tap water. Furthermore, Co2+ in human urine was detected by using the probes with recoveries between 96% and 104%, indicating that the established probes could meet the requirements of practical detection in urine samples. Besides, the probes were successfully applied to monitoring Co2+ in A549 cells via fluorescence imaging attributed to their excellent photostability and high signal-to-background ratio. All these results show that the designed B,N-CQDs are ideal probes for simultaneously detecting Co2+ in environmental water and biological samples, which would expand the practical application of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is considerably used in battery manufacturing, automotive industries, metal finishing, and so on, which may cause cobalt ions (Co2+) residue in natural environment [1]. High level of Co2+ in water may continuously escalate its toxicity as Co2+ being accumulated by aquatic organisms to poison aquatic animals and plants [2]. Therefore, the permissible limits of Co2+ in irrigation watering and livestock watering are 0.05 and 1.0 mg·mL−1, respectively [3]. On the other hand, excessive intake of Co2+ is toxic to human body with increasing damage over time, causing asthma, heart failure and damaging to the thyroid and liver [4]. Correspondingly, individual intake of Co2+ is studied by determination of Co2+ concentration in urine and cells [5], particularly for occupationally cobalt-exposed workers. Consequently, to establish a versatile strategy for sensitive detection of Co2+ in water and biological samples is completely worthwhile.

Hitherto, several techniques including atomic absorption spectroscopy [6,7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8], UV–vis spectrophotometry [9], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering have been explored for detecting Co2+ [10]. Nevertheless, the necessity of time-consuming separation procedures, sophisticated detection protocols, costly preparation property and unsatisfactory detection sensitivity, significantly limit wide application of these techniques, especially in rapid and sensitive detection. Fluorescence, featuring operational simplicity, rapid response, excellent sensitivity, high repeatability, and local observation, has become a predominant tool in environmental monitoring and life science [11]. In particular, ascribed to their excellent water solubility, outstanding optical stability and ease of modification, a variety of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based fluorescence probes have been established for detecting Co2+ in water [12]. However, the allowable maximum content of Co2+ in environmental water is at nanomole level and the LOD of vast majority of these reported CQDs is at micromole level, which cannot achieve directly determination of Co2+ in actual water samples [13]. To improve detection sensitivity, one commonly used strategy is to add capture reagent into water samples to bond Co2+ by forming complex compound first, then the fluorescence of CQDs was quenched by the formed compound via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [14]. However, the addition of extra reagents may cause complication of the detection system and lower detection efficiency. Therefore, synthesis of CQDs-based probes with excellent sensitivity to directly detect trace Co2+ in water samples is still highly desirable. In addition to water samples, it is challenging to determinate Co2+ in biological samples. As is well-known, the components in biological samples are more complicated than those in water samples, which gives rise to an intimidating hurdle toward the selective capture and sensitive detection of Co2+. For human urine samples, the coexisting matrix substances (metal ions, proteins, amino acids) may competitively interact with CQDs via thiol group and weaken the interaction between Co2+ and CQDs, leading to signal loss of Co2+ [15]. Besides, the allowable range of Co2+ in human urine is from 1.7 nM to 37.3 nM, which requires the CQDs-based probes with rather low LOD for detection [16]. These may be why CQDs implemented as probes to monitor Co2+ in human urine has not been reported yet. For identification of Co2+ in living cells, except matrix interference, the excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and high FLQY of CQDs are other critical factors needed to be considered. As well known, FLQY is an essential property for bioimaging, which allow sensitive and long-term imaging of target analytes. However, FLQY of mostly reported CQDs are poor (<40%), which has become the bottleneck in further development of high contrast and resolution in cell imaging [17]. Besides, both excitation and emission of mostly reported CQDs for sensing Co2+ in cells are mainly in purple and blue wavelength region, where the fluorescence signal of CQDs is dramatically affected by strong autofluorescence, resulting in low resolution of bioimaging [18]. Literature research discloses that there is only a single example for the application of N-CQDs emitting green light in detection of Co2+ via fluorescence imaging [19], while the FLQY is 24%. Furthermore, excitation in the UV range (300–400 nm) may generate severe photodamage for cells during imaging [20]. Therefore, preparation of CQDs with excellent recognition capability of Co2+, high FLQY and long emission wavelength can afford a reliable solution to the problem in fluorescence determination of Co2+ in different real samples.

Herein, we report the fabrication of novel boron, nitrogen-doped CQDs (B,N-CQDs) via one-pot hydrothermal method, serving as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitively monitoring Co2+ in environmental water and biological samples. The synthetic and detecting route of the probes are outlined in Scheme 1 . 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPBA) and ethylenediamine (ED) were designedly selected as precursors to synthesize B,N-CQDs due to their advantageous features: 1) excellent water-solubility was benefit for fully reaction during hydrothermal synthesis to raise yield; 2) containing large amounts of N and B for generating doped CQDs to improve FLQY; 3) intrinsically carried carboxyl and amino groups to specifically capture Co2+. All these properties offer the obtained B,N-CQDs attractive advantageous as fluorescence probes. When Co2+ exists, green-emitting probes can be effectively quenched through the metal-ligand interaction between Co2+ and the surface groups of the probes. Finally, attributed to its outstanding optical stability, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and high FLQY, the probes were successfully used to monitor Co2+ not only in various environmental water samples, including plant effluent, river water as well as tap water, but also in human urine and cell samples.

For detailed information about materials and instruments, please see Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI, Section 1, 2). B,N-CQDs were synthesized as follows: CPBA (0.0497 g) and ED (80 μL) were mixed in distilled water (30 mL) and sonicated for 20 mins. Then the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined auto-clave and heated at 240 °C for 4 h. After being naturally cooled to room temperature, the yellow crude product was collected through a 0.22 µm micro-pore film filter to remove the raw stuff and the large-sized CQDs. Ultimately, B,N-CQDs were dialyzed against ultrapure water to keep out impurities through a dialysis membrane (MWAG of 100 Da) for 2 days, during which time ultrapure water was changed with 8 h interval. Then the B,N-CQDs solution (5 mg·mL−1) was conserved at 4 ℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ by the probes
ce:section.ce:para: The B,N-CQDs solution was directly used as fluorescence probes. To assess the sensitivity of the probes, the quenching effect of Co2+ on fluorescence intensity of the probes was conducted as follows: 3 mL of the B,N-CQDs solution (500 μg·mL−1, in PBS buffer (0.001 M, pH 7.0)) was placed in a 5.0 mL quartz cuvette. The fluorescence emission intensity at 509 nm of the blank sample (the probes without adding Co2+) excited at 412 nm was measured and marked as F 0. Next, a certain amount of Co2+ stock solution was diluted with pure water and continuously added 15 μL into the quartz cuvette each time to the final concentration in the range of 0.5–1.0 × 108 nM. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 10 s, allowed to rest for 1 min at room temperature, and measured the fluorescent intensity (F). The reproducibility of the probes was research to get the error rod, when repeat number, n, was 3 or more.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and anti-interference assay of the probes
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the probes was studied as follows: initially, eighteen different ions (including Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Li+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Sr2+, Cr3+, SO4 2−, NO3 −), and some other molecules were equipped with a solution (0.01 M) for further use. Afterwards, 900 μL of the probes with concentration of 500 μg·mL−1 was added into each centrifuge tube and then a volume of 100 μL of the prepared solution above was respectively added into tubes to reach the final concentration of 1 mM. Later, all tubes were shaken thoroughly for 10 s and transferred to a spectrophotometer quartz cuvette ready for fluorescence spectra recording after 1 min at room temperature.

The anti-interference property of the probes was investigated as follows: different potent interferences solution (0.01 M) were prepared, including (a) different ions including Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Li+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Sr2+, Cr3+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, (b) various amino acids like GSH, Tyr, Ala, Leu, Trp, Ile, Arg, Gly, Hcy, Cys, Thr, Phe, Lys and etc., and (c) other molecules in urine such as C4H7N3O, CH4N2O, KCl, Na2SO4, NaCl, MgSO4·7H2O, CaSO4·2H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O and so on. As control group, 100 μL Co2+ (0.01 M) was added into 900 μL of the probes (500 μg·mL−1) to get the final concentration of 1 mM, which was 10-fold lower than the concentration of various interferences. The same assay procedure was conducted and the fluorescence spectra at 412 nm excitation were measured.

In addition, interference analysis of matrix components in urine were simulated by detecting Co2+ in artificial human urine (more details of sample preparation in ESI, Section 1). Firstly, 900 μL of the probes (dissolved in pure water, 500 μg·mL−1) was added into two centrifuge tubes, and then 100 μL of water and Co2+ (10 μM) were added respectively into relevant tubes to attain the final concentration of 1 μM. Correspondingly, 100 μL of water and Co2+ (10 μM) were respectively put into 900 μL of the probes (dissolved in artificial urine solution, 500 μg·mL−1) in each tube to get the final concentration of 1 μM. At last, the same assay procedure was conducted and the fluorescence spectra were measured after 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Co2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For details about real samples, please see Section 1 in ESI. For environmental water samples: The industrial wastewater was diluted 5-fold for further use. First, the water samples (Yudai River, tap water of laboratory and industrial wastewater) were spiked with different amounts of Co2+ standard solution. 3 mL of the B,N-CQDs solution (500 μg·mL−1, pH 7.0) was added into a tube, then 15 μL of the spiked water samples was added into the tube and incubated for 1 min. Immediately, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture was recorded. Each measurement was done in triplicate for calculating the corresponding recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD). For human urine samples: Various amounts of Co2+ standard solution was spiked into the urine samples. 10 μL of the spiked sample was added into 3 mL of the B,N-CQDs solution (500 μg·mL−1, pH 7.0), and then the same assay procedure was conducted as above.

For cell samples: The A549 cells (5 × 104 cells) were cultured in a Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM)-supplemented solution containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin in each hole for 24 h. DMEM medium was used to prepare the B,N-CQDs solution with a concentration of 200 μg·mL−1, which contained different concentrations ofCo2+ (0, 1 µM and 1 mM). Then, 0.1 mL of the suspension was added to cells, and the cells were incubated for another 24 h at 37 ℃ under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The excess probes were removed by rinsing three times with fresh PBS (pH = 7.4). Eventually, the samples were fixed on the slide for analysis by High Content Analysis System Operetta CLS™.

B,N-CQDs were facile synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method, in which CPBA and ED were selected as precursors. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. 1 (a)) shows the well morphology of B,N-CQDs, which are monodispersed and uniformly spherical nanoparticles. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals the high crystallinity of B,N-CQDs (inserted in Fig. 1(a)). The lattice spacing of B,N-CQDs is observed approximately 0.24 nm, resembling the (100) lattice fringes of graphite [21]. The size of B,N-CQDs increases from 1.3 nm to 4.0 nm, displaying a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 2.5 nm via Gaussian fitting (Fig. 1(b)), which is suitable for bio-applications [22].

Doping heteroatoms into CQDs could significantly affect their intrinsic properties such as FLQY, emission spectra shifting, and local chemical features [23]. XPS and FT-IR were used to analyze the surface composition and the functional groups of B,N-CQDs, which explored whether B and N atoms were successfully doped. The full XPS spectrum of B,N-CQDs in Fig. S1 (ESI) presents four peaks around 286.3 eV, 533.0 eV, 401.2 eV, 192.5 eV probably ascribed to the binding energies of C1s, O1s, N1s and B1s. In Fig. 1(c), the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s consists of five peaks, which reveal five different environments of carbon, corresponding to C–B at 283.8 eV, sp2 C from CC/CC at 284.6 eV, sp3 C from CN/CO at 285.4 eV, CN at 286.9 eV, and CO at 288.6 eV [24]. Besides, the O 1 s spectrum manifests that the peaks at 532.4 and 531.3 eV are for C = O and C–O in Fig. 1(d) [25]. Additionally, the high resolution scan at the N 1 s region can be fitted into two peaks at 401.4 and 400.0 eV in Fig. 1(e), which can be assigned as N − H and C − N bonds [26]. B 1 s core-level peaks appear at 192.8 and 192.1 eV in Fig. 1(f), which are assigned to the BO and BC bonds, respectively [27]. As for the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1(g)), carboxyl groups are clearly identified, through the broad OH stretching vibration (3391 cm−1) and the sharp CO and CO stretching vibration (1684 and 1029 cm−1) in curve (a) [28]. Besides, peaks at 1365, 1174 and 779 cm−1 are attributed to the BO stretching, BOH bending mode and vibration of OBO most likely from B(OH)2 group [29]. A typical peak at 1483 cm−1 is originated from the structure of CN, while the band centered at 3347 cm−1 is derived from NH in curve (b) [30]. Distinctly, the spectrum of B,N-CQDs is indeed different from reactant. Peaks at 3423, 1661 and 1546 cm−1 correspond to OH/NH, CO stretching vibrations and N − H bending vibration [31]. Although the vibration of BO (located at 1395 cm−1) still exists in curve (c), the vibration of OBO as well as BOH becomes significantly weak, meaning that part B(OH)2 group decreases but still exists on the surface of B,N-CQDs even after hydrothermal reaction [32]. Not only these changes, but also the vibration of OH or NH has the same variation tendency. Overall, these results demonstrate the successful reaction between CPBA and ED, indicating that B and N atoms have been doped into CQDs.

Obvious absorption peaks are focused on 236 and 311 nm in the UV/Vis spectrum of B,N-CQDs (Fig. 1(h)), which are assigned to the n–π* transition of the CO band and the π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band, while another transition centered at 402 nm is due to trapping of excited state energy by surface states [33]. Symmetrical fluorescence spectra of B,N-CQDs are observed that a peak of emission spectra is at 509 nm upon excitation at 412 nm, which is close to the absorption band in the lower energy region. Excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of B,N-CQDs (Fig. 1(h)) may be attributed to surface states affecting the band gap of CQDs [34]. Essentially, the surface state is analogous to a molecular state whereas the size effect results from quantum dimensions, both of which contribute to the complexity of the excited states of CQDs [35]. The absolute FLQY was determined to be 64.8% by the absolute method (details of FLQY measurements in ESI, Section 3) [36], exceeding the vast majority of reported CQDs, which could promote the application in sensitive detection and bio-imaging. It is confirmed the B,N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of CQDs, that is to say, doping N and B elements may alter the structure and increase FLQY of B,N-CQDs [31].

As designed fluorescence probes, the fluorescence stability of B,N-CQDs including the effects of the ionic strength, pH and UV exposure were evaluated. Great ionic strength tolerance of fluorescence probes is indispensable to practical applications in the presence of environmental and physical salt concentrations. In Fig. S2(a) (ESI), there was scarcely no signal variation in fluorescence intensity of the probes with serial concentrations of NaCl ranging from 0 to 1.0 M, indicating the probes possess superior capability in anti-ion interference. Likewise, the normalized intensity of the probes mainly fluctuated in the minuscule range when the pH value changed from 2 to 13 (Fig. S2(b), ESI), displaying the excellent optical stability of the probes even under extreme pH conditions. The normalized intensity of the probes decreased slightly with duration of UV excitation even after 60 mins (Fig. S2(c), ESI), verifying their great photostability. None of the tested conditions significantly altered the fluorescence intensity of the probes after long-term storage (30 days) at room temperature in natural light (Fig. S2(d), ESI). Advantageously, the powder-like B,N-CQDs might be repeatedly solved in water with good dispersibility but no aggregation, which was to the benefit of store and use. All these studies indicate that the probes can be appropriate for practical detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions of the probes
ce:section.ce:para: Influential factors, such as pH, buffer, reaction time and concentration of probes, are essential for the practical detection of Co2+. The influence of pH and buffer during the detection of Co2+ were discussed (Figs. S3 and S4, ESI), showing the best quenching efficiency at pH 7.0 and in PBS buffer (pH 7.0, 0.001 M). The fluorescence of the probes decreased rapidly when Co2+ was introduced and quenching equilibrium was attained in 1 min (Fig. S5, ESI). The quenching efficiency of the probes increased sluggishly ranging from 0.3 to 0.5 mg·mL−1 due to the self-quenching effect, and then reached the peak at 0.5 mg·mL−1 (Fig. S6, ESI). As a result, 0.5 mg·mL−1 of the probes in PBS buffer (pH 7.0, 0.001 M) and reaction period of 1 min were chosen for the next experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ by the probes
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the analytical performance of the probes, the fluorescence titrations were carried out to plot the relationship between the concentration of Co2+ and the degree of fluorescent quenching (F0-F). Fig. 2 (a) shows the response of the probes toward Co2+ at various concentrations from 0.5 to 1.0 × 108 nM. Under the excitation wavelength at 412 nm, the fluorescent signals of the probes at 509 nm were weakened progressively when Co2+ was added. Excellent linearities (y = 3.170x + 13.722, y = 0.329x + 62.715) over the ranges from 0.5 to 10 nM and 10 to 2000 nM with correlation coefficient squares (R2) 0.997 and 0.994 were obtained (Fig. 2(b) and Fig. 2(c)), for which LOD of 0.52 nM was derived (LOD = 3δb /s, δb is the standard deviation of the blank samples (δb = 0.54943), and s means slope of this equation (y = 3.170x + 13.722, s = 3.170)). Compared with previous reports (Table 1 ), B,N-CQDs-based probes exhibited higher sensitivity and wider detection range than reported CQDs.

Besides, selectivity assay of the probes toward Co2+ was discussed. As depicted in Fig. 2(d), the fluorescence signal of the probes was remarkably quenched only in the presence of Co2+, whereas no significant fluorescence quenching was noticed with other ions. The effect of the probes on another competing chemical species in environmental and biological samples was determined (Figs. S7 and S8, ESI), which was observed the remarkable quenching of the probes only toward Co2+. Moreover, the influence of matrix components in artificial urine solution were discussed. Satisfactorily, the interferent basically affected neither the fluorescence intensity of the probes nor the whole detection system (B,N-CQDs-Co2+ system) (Fig. S9, ESI). In short, the probes, featuring high capability of Co2+, good anti-interference ability and optical stability, possess wide applicable prospects in detection of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the probes for detection of Co2+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection mechanism of the probes was discussed. It was reported that several molecular interactions could lead to fluorescence quenching, for example excited-state reactions, molecular rearrangements, energy transfer, ground-state complex formation, and collisional quenching [31]. And there are two major categories of quenching mechanism: dynamic and static quenching, which can be discovered by dint of fluorescence lifetime and its disparity [37]. As the fluorescence lifetime can be shortened during the process of dynamic quenching, while be invariant in static one. Fig. 2(e) presents that the average fluorescence lifetime of the probes virtually stays the same even adding 0.1 or 1 mM Co2+ solution, illustrating that the fluorescence mechanism of the probes belongs to the static quenching. Besides, UV–vis absorption spectrum is also an available way to confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism, seeing as the unchanging absorption spectra of collisional quenching before and after quenching. Thus, unlike the UV–vis spectrum of B,N-CQDs-Co2+ solution after fluorescence quenching, the summed UV–vis absorption spectrum of B,N-CQDs and Co2+ varies enormously, which elucidates the coordinated bond between Co2+ and carboxyl and amino groups in the surface of B,N-CQDs (Fig. 2(f)). To sum up, it is interpreted that the fluorescence behavior of the B,N-CQDs-Co2+ system is a type of the static fluorescence quenching.Perhaps the nonradiative electron may transfer from the excited states to the d orbital of Co2+ in the process of fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the versatility of the fluorescence probes for detecting Co2+, we detected three kinds of real samples: water samples, human urine and cells. The probes were used for monitoring Co2+ in environmental water samples, including Yudai River from Changsha (China), tap water of laboratory and industrial downstream wastewater from local factory. Real water samples without complex pretreatment spiked with different concentrations of Co2+ were analyzed using the probes (Table 2 ). Co2+ was not detectable in tap water due to the great water treatment of waterworks, but it was quantified with concentration of 2.26 and 1.25 μM in sewage water and river water, respectively. The results show that the recovery rate is 96–106% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 2.4–4.3%, indicating that the probes can meet the requirements of practical detection in water samples. Furthermore, to confirm the practicability of the probes, the obtained results from water samples were compared with those determined by Inductively Coupled Plasma-Mass Spectrometry (ICP − MS) in Table S1 (ESI). The differences between the results obtained by our probes and ICP − MS technique are very tiny, which exemplifies the high accuracy and potential applicability of the probes for detecting Co2+ in real water samples. Prominently, our probes for detecting Co2+ are more rapid and facile, circumventing many drawbacks such as complex sample pretreatment and instrumentation by using ICP-MS.

Although tiny Co2+ is ingested at a time from environment water, the amount of Co2+ accumulated cannot be ignored in the human body. In general, analysis result can be obtained by measuring Co2+ in urine due to non-invasive sampling and easier collection. In this work, the probes were applied to the analysis of Co2+ in human urine from Xiangya Hospital. In Table 3 , Co2+ was detected with the concentration of 10.5 and 4.3 nM in urine samples from 50-year old male and 25-year-old male respectively, both of which were within the normal content range of Co2+ in urine. Even more, the reason why Co2+ content from 50-year old male was twice as much as that from 25-year-old male was speculated. Smokers have a higher mean cobalt concentration in urine than the nonsmokers, since tobacco smoke contains 0.5% cobalt [16]. Whereas Co2+ was not identified in urine sample from 22-year-old female, we could speculate that there was very little Co2+ within this urine sample. The results show satisfied recoveries in the range of 96–104 % and RSDs ranging from 2.1% to 4.3%, suggesting that complex matrix in urine has little effect on the detection performance of the probes. Thus, the probes were successful applied to determine trace Co2+ in human urine. Furthermore, it can also be extended to detect Co2+ in urine samples from occupationally exposed workers.

Studies suggested that Co2+ is able to stimulate the generation of reactive oxygen species causing oxidative stress leading to DNA base damage and strand breaks in different cells. Accordingly, sensitive determination of Co2+ in cells via fluorescence imaging is of great significance. Prior to cellular imaging, the cytotoxicity of the probes was investigated by CCK-8 assay (details of cell viability assay in ESI, Section 4). After 24 h incubation, the cell viability rates of A549 cells maintained above 95% when the concentration of the probes was from 10 to 200 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 3 (a)). Combined, 200.0 µg·mL−1 was chosen as the concentration of the probes in next experiments. Here, Fig. 3(b–d) represented the bright-field, fluorescent-field, and overlay images of the A549 cells without adding Co2+, captured by High Content Analysis System Operetta CLS™. The probes readily permeabilized and transferred into live cells without significant changes in cell morphology, which revealed their excellent biocompatibility. To explore the application of the probes for semiquantitative monitoring of Co2+ in cells, exogenous Co2+ was introduced into the probes-pretreated A549 cells. Fig. 3(e, h), (f, i) and (g, j) separately revealed the bright-field, fluorescent-field, and overlay images of the A549 cells, which were treated with the probes and different concentration ofCo2+ (1 µM and 1 mM) for 24 h. Initially, the probes showed clearly green fluorescence in cells without adding Co2+, but their emitting light became weaker even almost vanished, as the concentration of Co2+ increased. All these results indicate that the probes with low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility are potent for specific recognition of Co2+ in cells.

In summary, we have successfully constructed versatile fluorescence probes (B,N-CQDs) for simultaneous detection of Co2+ in environmental water, human urine and cells. The green fluorescence probes are fabricated via one-step hydrothermal treatment of CPBA and ED, which possess the high FLQY (64.8%) and excellent specific recognition capacity of Co2+, endowing these designed probes with outstanding sensitivity and accuracy of analysis. Significantly, for the first time, the probes are used for the quantitative detection of Co2+ in human urine. On the premise of good water solubility, optical stability and nontoxicity, the probes are applied to cell imaging. Moreover, due to the versatility of the probes, various samples in practical detection can be readily expanded including drugs, food and so on. We believe that these versatile fluorescence probes would find wide applications in environmental analysis, food safety and drug administration.

Xinyi Zhao: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Lumin Wang: Writing - review & editing. Qi Liu: Supervision. Miao Chen: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Xiaoqing Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878339, 21904141), the Key R&D Project in the industrial field funded by Hunan Provincial Science &Technology Department, China (2019GK2131), and Independent Exploration and Innovation Project for Graduate Students of Central South University, China (2020zzts055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105888.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
